era_context: The eve of the Seven Years' War; European powers are mobilizing for conflict. The Lisbon earthquake on 1 November 1755 is a watershed moment in European intellectual history, challenging Enlightenment optimism. The Qing Dynasty continues its consolidation of power in China. The Ottoman Empire faces internal challenges. Colonial rivalries in North America, India, and the Caribbean are reaching a critical point.

events.title: Lisbon earthquake and tsunami
events.description: On 1 November 1755 (All Saints' Day), a massive earthquake with an estimated magnitude of 8.5–9.0 strikes Lisbon, Portugal. The earthquake is followed by a tsunami and fires that destroy approximately 85% of the city's buildings. Estimates of deaths range from 10,000 to 100,000, with modern scholarship favoring figures around 15,000–30,000. The disaster kills approximately 15% of Lisbon's population and destroys irreplaceable cultural, religious, and architectural monuments. The earthquake has profound philosophical consequences across Europe, challenging Enlightenment optimism about reason and divine benevolence. Voltaire's Poem on the Lisbon Disaster (1756) and Rousseau's response exemplify the intellectual ferment. Reconstruction under the Marquês de Pombal becomes a model of rational urban planning and administrative reform.
key_figures: Marquês de Pombal (Portuguese Prime Minister, oversees reconstruction)
Voltaire (French philosopher, responds to the disaster)

sources.name: Contemporary eyewitness accounts and letters from Lisbon
Voltaire, Poem on the Lisbon Disaster
Geological and seismic analysis of the 1755 event
Rui Pena Pires, The Lisbon Earthquake of 1755: Contested Narratives, Materialities, Consequences

events.certainty_note: The earthquake date of 1 November 1755 is certain from contemporary sources. The magnitude and death toll are estimates based on historical accounts and modern seismic analysis, with some uncertainty in the exact figures.

events.title: Battle of the Monongahela (Braddock's Defeat)
events.description: On 9 July 1755, British General Edward Braddock is defeated and mortally wounded in an ambush by French forces and their Native American allies near the Monongahela River in present-day Pennsylvania. Braddock's force of approximately 1,500 British regulars and colonial militia is routed by a smaller French and indigenous force using guerrilla tactics. The defeat is a major setback for British colonial ambitions in North America and demonstrates the vulnerability of European linear tactics to irregular warfare. Braddock dies of his wounds on 13 July 1755. The battle becomes a symbol of British military overconfidence and the need for adaptation to colonial warfare.
key_figures: General Edward Braddock (British commander, mortally wounded)
George Washington (colonial officer, present at the battle)
Captain Daniel-Hyacinthe-Marie Liénard de Beaujeu (French commander)

sources.name: George Washington's account and letters
British military records and court of inquiry
French military reports
Francis Parkman, Montcalm and Wolfe

events.certainty_note: Battle date of 9 July 1755 and Braddock's death on 13 July are well-documented in contemporary military records and Washington's correspondence.

events.title: Expulsion of the Acadians begins
events.description: In 1755, British colonial authorities begin the forced expulsion of French-speaking Acadians from Nova Scotia and surrounding regions. The expulsion, known as the Dérangement or Great Upheaval, is driven by British fears of Acadian loyalty to France during the Seven Years' War. Over the next several years, approximately 10,000–18,000 Acadians are forcibly removed from their homes, with many dying during transport or in exile. Families are separated, and communities are destroyed. Survivors scatter to Louisiana, the Caribbean, and other locations. The expulsion is one of the earliest examples of ethnic cleansing in North American history and has lasting cultural and demographic consequences.
key_figures: Governor Charles Lawrence (Nova Scotia, orders the expulsion)
Colonel John Winslow (British officer, oversees deportations)

sources.name: British colonial records and military orders
Acadian oral traditions and later accounts
Naomi Griffiths, From Migrant to Acadian: A North American Border People, 1604–1755

events.certainty_note: The expulsion began in 1755 and continued through the early 1760s. The date and general facts are well-documented in British colonial records, though the full scale and human cost were long minimized in English-language historiography.
cross_references: 1763-001 (Treaty of Paris ends the Seven Years' War, affecting Acadian diaspora)

events.title: Qing Dynasty consolidates control over Dzungar Khanate
events.description: In 1755, the Qing Dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor completes its military conquest and consolidation of the Dzungar Khanate in Central Asia. The Dzungars, a Mongol confederation, had resisted Qing expansion for decades. The final conquest involves both military campaigns and the systematic resettlement of Han Chinese and other populations into the region. The conquest expands Qing territorial control to its greatest extent and secures the Silk Road trade routes. The region is renamed Xinjiang ('New Frontier'). The conquest has long-term demographic and cultural consequences, as Han Chinese settlement increases and Dzungar political autonomy is eliminated.
key_figures: Amursana (Dzungar leader, initially cooperates with Qing, later rebels)

sources.name: Qing imperial records and military reports
Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) and later Chinese chronicles
Peter C. Perdue, China Marches West: The Qing Conquest of Central Eurasia

events.certainty_note: The conquest of the Dzungar Khanate is documented in Qing imperial records. The date of 1755 marks the completion of the main military campaigns, though consolidation continued afterward.

events.title: Samuel Johnson publishes A Dictionary of the English Language
events.description: On 15 April 1755, Samuel Johnson publishes his monumental A Dictionary of the English Language, the result of nine years of labor. The dictionary contains approximately 42,000 entries and is the most comprehensive English dictionary produced to date. Johnson's work becomes the standard reference for English language and usage for nearly two centuries, until the Oxford English Dictionary supersedes it in the late 19th century. The dictionary reflects Johnson's literary judgment and includes illustrative quotations from English literature. It becomes a symbol of English cultural achievement during the Enlightenment.
key_figures: Lord Chesterfield (patron, though Johnson famously rejects his late patronage)

sources.name: Samuel Johnson, A Dictionary of the English Language
Johnson's letters and biographical accounts
James Boswell, The Life of Samuel Johnson

events.certainty_note: Publication date of 15 April 1755 is documented in contemporary records and Johnson's correspondence.

events.title: Pombal's reforms in Portugal begin
events.description: Following the Lisbon earthquake, the Marquês de Pombal, Prime Minister of Portugal, initiates sweeping administrative and economic reforms. These reforms include the reconstruction of Lisbon on a rational grid plan, the modernization of the Portuguese military and navy, the reduction of the power of the Catholic Church and the Inquisition, and the promotion of commerce and industry. Pombal's reforms are among the most ambitious enlightened absolutist policies in 18th-century Europe. They transform Portugal's administrative structure and lay the groundwork for economic modernization, though they also involve the suppression of the Jesuits and increased state control over the Church.

sources.name: Portuguese royal decrees and administrative records
Pombal's correspondence and reports
Kenneth Maxwell, Pombal: Paradox of the Enlightenment

events.certainty_note: Pombal's reforms began in earnest following the 1755 earthquake and are documented in Portuguese administrative records.

disconfirming_evidence: The death toll from the Lisbon earthquake is often cited as 100,000 or more in older sources, but modern scholarship, based on demographic analysis and contemporary accounts, suggests figures closer to 15,000–30,000. The earthquake's philosophical impact is sometimes overstated; while it did challenge Enlightenment optimism, many Enlightenment thinkers (including some who criticized Voltaire's pessimism) maintained faith in reason and progress. The Acadian expulsion is sometimes portrayed as a purely military necessity, but modern historians argue it was driven by ethnic and religious prejudice as much as strategic concerns. The Qing conquest of the Dzungar Khanate is sometimes presented as a straightforward military victory, but the process involved complex diplomacy, internal divisions among the Dzungars, and later rebellions that complicate the narrative of simple conquest.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1755 is rich for European and colonial events, particularly the Lisbon earthquake and the early stages of the Seven Years' War. However, the perspective is heavily weighted toward European sources. Chinese sources for the Qing conquest of the Dzungar Khanate are available but less frequently consulted in Western historiography. The Acadian expulsion is documented in British colonial records but was long minimized or ignored in English-language histories; Acadian and French-Canadian sources provide important counternarratives. The intellectual impact of the Lisbon earthquake is well-documented in European philosophical and literary sources, but the perspectives of Portuguese common people and the broader Mediterranean world are less well represented. Indigenous American perspectives on the Battle of the Monongahela and the broader colonial conflict are underrepresented in the historical record.

graph_edges.note: The Lisbon earthquake triggers Pombal's comprehensive reforms of Portuguese administration and society
Braddock's Defeat demonstrates the need for British military adaptation and contributes to the escalation that leads to the formal declaration of the Seven Years' War in 1756
The Acadian expulsion is part of the broader Seven Years' War conflict and its aftermath
The Qing conquest of the Dzungar Khanate is followed by Amursana's rebellion in 1757
